questions.question: What is the primary technical problem that CSS Modules solve in React?
options: Compiling Sass variables to CSS
Eliminating global CSS namespace collisions by hashing class names at build time
Extracting all styles into a single minified file
Enabling dark mode across the application
questions.answer: Eliminating global CSS namespace collisions by hashing class names at build time
questions.explanation: CSS Modules automatically scopes CSS classes locally to the component. The class '.button' is transformed at build time into something unique like '.Button_module_button__HASH', so it cannot conflict with other '.button' classes in the app.

questions.question: What is a major downside of using runtime CSS-in-JS libraries (like Styled Components or Emotion) in modern React (Server Components)?
options: They do not support CSS animations
They incur a runtime performance penalty and do not easily support React Server Components
They require developers to learn a new syntax that isn't CSS
They cannot accept React props
questions.answer: They incur a runtime performance penalty and do not easily support React Server Components
questions.explanation: Because libraries like Styled Components parse styles and inject <style> tags at runtime in the browser, they block React Server Components from streaming directly and cause a performance hit.

questions.question: What is Tailwind CSS primarily considered to be?
options: A component library like Bootstrap (pre-built buttons, cards)
A CSS-in-JS library
A Utility-First CSS framework providing low-level classes to build designs in JSX
A React hook for managing CSS variables
questions.answer: A Utility-First CSS framework providing low-level classes to build designs in JSX
questions.explanation: Tailwind provides granular classes like 'bg-red-500' and 'flex' rather than highly opinionated components like 'card' or 'btn'. This allows rapid, custom component development without leaving JSX or writing raw CSS.

questions.question: Why is the clsx (or classnames) utility library commonly used in React projects?
options: To compile CSS into Webpack
To conditionally construct HTML classname strings safely based on JS objects and arrays
To replace the styled-components library
To polyfill CSS Grid in older browsers
questions.answer: To conditionally construct HTML classname strings safely based on JS objects and arrays
questions.explanation: Constructing dynamic classes using template literals (className={`${base} ${active ? 'active' : ''}`}) gets messy quickly with hanging spaces. clsx({ active: isActive }) handles the logic cleanly.

questions.question: What does the tailwind-merge library specifically solve when creating reusable components?
options: It minifies the final CSS bundle automatically
It prevents class collisions by smartly overriding conflicting Tailwind utility classes
It merges Tailwind configs from multiple repositories
It converts Tailwind classes into inline styles
questions.answer: It prevents class collisions by smartly overriding conflicting Tailwind utility classes
questions.explanation: If a component defaults to 'px-2 py-1 bg-red-500', and a user passes 'bg-blue-500', they might render 'bg-red-500 bg-blue-500'. CSS cascade (not class order) determines the winner, causing bugs. tailwind-merge removes 'bg-red-500' if 'bg-blue-500' is appended.
